item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah that's a typo, thanks for the correction XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was silly and forgot to put an edit reason, but I corrected a typo in the containment procedures - avian lifefroms to lifeforms. If that's not correct feel free to revert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As stated in the author post, this is largely based/inspired by on a Chinese myth. I don't think I've ever read 020-JP, and I believe this is just a coincidence of using similar elements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh? A girl with wings for arms and suffered from osteoporosis? I'm pretty sure it's just coincidence, but that just sound like simpler version of SCP-020-JP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good thing this number's occupied by this skip rather than another one about flesh monsters. I was expecting something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't know the myth but I like how it hinted and implied at a story rather than directly telling the story. I think this is really brilliant. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ohhhhh that would be pretty great! Looking forward to it :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. For me, at least, the previous knowledge somehow makes me dislike the SCP. Having recited the myth in Chinese class, I really feel that the story is too simple or lacks twists for me to upvote it.

item.title: Re: Sorry but downvote
item.content:encoded: The issue here is that this one feels a bit underwhelming, but I must admit that what you say is true.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple, Succinct and clear. Great Work sir! :) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad that it is up on the mainlist. As stated in a previous correspondence, if the situation is favourable, I think it's time for an SCP about formerly undersea palaces within that region – once I figure out how that can work out.

item.title: Re: Sorry but downvote
item.content:encoded:
I prefer skips with a high "HOLY SH*T" quotient, and/or with twisted and clever narratives. Here, it's just…boring, uninteresting.

If everyone of the over 3000 SCPs follow that exact narrative format, it will be the entire SCP concept that will become boring and uninteresting. Variety adds flavour.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt like I was missing something by the time I got to the end of this, and I think that something was foreknowledge of the myth.

Which, now that I know it, this seems a fairly straightforward interpretation thereof. Not sure if upvote. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, I just pulled one of my SCP's down. I'll get the writing process eventually. Great job though!

item.title: Sorry but downvote
item.content:encoded:
I do realise I'm in the minority here, but I really don't like this skip. I prefer skips with a high "HOLY SH*T" quotient, and/or with twisted and clever narratives. Here, it's just…boring, uninteresting.

-1 downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While learning the myth added another layer, I enjoyed the SCP on its own. It was anomalous and definitely unsettling, and was neutralized without any gory gooey death happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just came back to this and realised I accidentally down voted when I meant to upvote >w<

But yeah, great work +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure this one really relies that much on previous knowledge of the myth, personally. Like, if you knew the myth beforehand that'll probably add some more wrinkles of meaning, but I do think the information present in the article can form a coherent (and meaningful) story:

There was a girl who got lost on a lake.

Her kin came to rescue her. They came and came and came.

It was too late, though.

It's been too late for five thousand years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah I don't really expect people to get the myth, and it's not intended to be massive. I just want it to be short and simple, and kinda sad? But if you don't like it, that's fine too.

I just want to convey the idea that a girl drowned here, and she wanted to remove the water that caused her death. And hopefully now she's kinda at peace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a fan of basing an SCP on a myth if you have to be familiar with the myth for the SCP to be interesting. Like, SCP-3000 works because, even without the legend, it's still a massive underwater serpent that eats people, messes with brains in interesting ways, and provides the Foundation with a vital resource. I have no idea who or what Anantashesha is, but that's okay because it's great anyway. It also helps that the SCP itself discusses the myth and gives some context so anyone interested could go on google and learn more.

This one…without being familiar with the myth, it's just this odd patch of ground with water that dries up, some weird nesting thing with birds, and a weird skeleton.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a short one I feel like doing, based on a Chinese myth, again XD

Great thanks to minmin, dreamingbluedragon, Zhange, Orionbasher, Lex1nat0r, MrWrong, Icepick, weryllium, Dexanote, Hippo, Modern_Erasmus, DrShaw, Tufto, salvagebar, and all others who read the draft or helped with the writing.

+ Original Myth
- Original Myth

This is based on the story of 精卫填海: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jingwei

Essentially, Emperor Yan/Shen nong's daughter drowned at the sea, her spirit turns into a bird and seeks to fill the ocean one day by keep throwing pebbles into it.

+ Backstory
- Backstory

The idea is that, you can't really just remove the ocean by throwing stones into it, however long it lasts, because the water will not be reduced. Hence the skip happen in which this actually works :P

Here it's not her spirit turns into birds but rather, her dying wish twists reality. And because she thought throwing pebbles into the water body can actually fill it, the reality was twisted to meet that image.

And with her body removed, the conditions no longer met, and she's sort of at peace.

There's also a Emperor Yan connection that (not essential, and I don't really expect people to get this), since it is established in my Xia series that Emperor Yan is a fleshcrafter, the girl here was fleshcrafting herself to grow wings but failed.

But as this is not actually an important part of the article, so it could also be that she's just a bird person or she's just a reality bender :3

Followed Athenodora's suggestion and changed the addendum a bit, great thanks to her :3
